column3: The Etruscans establish city-states in central Italy (modern Tuscany, Umbria, Lazio).

column2: Tarsus is mentioned in Xenophon's Anabasis (around 400 BCE) as a great and wealthy city8.
The Proto-Turkic language, ancestor of all Turkic languages, is estimated to have originated in Asia around 500 BCE1.

column3: Major Etruscan city falls to Rome.
Rome defeats last major Etruscan coalition.

column2: The name "Türk" was first used as a political identity during the Göktürk Khaganate period in the 6th century CE7.

column4: Greek form of "Turk", entering European languages

column3: Tuscany ruled by Lombards, later by the Franks.

column2: Old Turkic, the first attested form of Turkic, has its earliest known records dating to the 7th century CE1.
The earliest written records of Turkish languages date back to the 8th century CE, found in the Orkhon Inscriptions9.
The linguistic history of Turkic languages can be followed in written sources from the 8th century CE onward10.

column3: Emergence of medieval city-states like Florence, Pisa, and Siena.
Toscano becomes the common name for the region.
